transcript: On the evening of November 28th, 2022 in Stock Island, Florida, Deputy Tanisha Multy was on a routine patrol when she noticed a man operating a black electric scooter bike with no lights.
The subject was wearing a long blonde wig, topped with a gray hat, and his bike had a distinct sticker on the back.
The following night, or rather, in the early morning hours of November 30th, she spotted the subject again, recognizable by his gray hat and the sticker on the back of the scooter.
The man still had no lights and was crossing a highway.
Deputy Tanisha and another deputy now en route, flashed their lights to conduct a traffic stop, but the man continued to ride the scooter over a bridge.
A stop like this is technically legal as an officer is only required to possess a reasonable suspicion that a traffic violation or crime has been committed.
In Florida, as of 2019, electric scooters are treated similarly to bicycles.

Like bicycles, they must follow certain rules of the road, and therefore the operator is subject to any violations that may arise, including the act of operating them in a manner that does not comply with the vehicle laws.
Scooters need to follow bike laws in between sunset and sunrise, bikes and scooters must display a front white light and a rear red reflector.
The subject pointed his finger toward the other side of the bridge, and the deputy assumed that he intended to stop there.
But things are about to take an unexpected turn.
Once over the bridge, the subject dropped his scooter and ran into a hotel garage and out towards the water.
